1: The command to consecrate the firstborn follows immediately after the first Passover — the redemption of Israel from Egypt is connected to the consecration of the firstborn. The structural link: YHWH struck Egypt's firstborn; therefore Israel's firstborn belong to YHWH. Every firstborn is a living memorial of the night of redemption. Colossians 1:15 identifies Jesus as 'the firstborn over all creation'; Revelation 1:5 as 'the firstborn from the dead.' The consecration of Israel's firstborn points to the true Firstborn whose consecration to death and resurrection is the ultimate ground of all redemption.
2: A type: 'Consecrate to me every firstborn — whatever first opens the womb... belongs to me.' The firstborn of every womb belongs to YHWH by right of redemption. Jesus is identified as the firstborn in four senses in the NT: firstborn over all creation (Colossians 1:15), firstborn from the dead (Colossians 1:18; Revelation 1:5), firstborn among many brothers (Romans 8:29), and specifically the firstborn presented at the temple (Luke 2:22-24, citing Ex 13:2 and Leviticus 12:8). Luke's account of the presentation of Jesus in the temple is the direct fulfillment of this very command — the true Firstborn is consecrated to YHWH in the prescribed manner.
3: 'Remember this day' — the Passover is a memorial feast grounded in a historical event of redemption. Theme: the Lord's Supper carries the same memorial structure. Jesus at the Last Supper says 'Do this in remembrance of me' (Luke 22:19; 1 Corinthians 11:24-25), establishing a new memorial feast grounded in a new redemptive event. As the Passover feast remembered the night of liberation from Egypt, the Lord's Supper remembers the night of liberation from sin. The pattern of redemption-event → covenant meal → annual/regular memorial is the same in both covenants.
4: The month of Abib (later Nisan) is consecrated as the memorial month — the calendar is organized around the redemption event. Theme: in Christ, the entire liturgical year is organized around his death and resurrection. The Christian calendar (Advent, Lent, Easter, Pentecost) is the Israelite sacred calendar rerouted through the cross. The Passover month becomes Holy Week; the first day after Passover becomes the day of resurrection (1 Corinthians 15:20: 'Christ has indeed been raised from the dead, the firstfruits of those who have fallen asleep').
5: When YHWH brings Israel into the promised land they are to observe this service — the feast of unleavened bread is forward-looking as well as backward-looking. A shadow: the Lord's Supper is similarly forward-looking — 'until he comes' (1 Corinthians 11:26). The memorial feast looks back to the redemption event, forward to the inheritance, and forward again to the return of the redeemer. Both feasts operate on this three-tense structure: what God did, where God is taking us, when God will complete it.
6: Seven days of eating unleavened bread — the complete period of the feast. 1 Corinthians 5:7-8 interprets this feast Christologically: 'Get rid of the old yeast, so that you may be a new unleavened batch — as you really are. For Christ, our Passover lamb, has been sacrificed. Therefore let us keep the Festival, not with the old bread leavened with malice and wickedness, but with the unleavened bread of sincerity and truth.' Paul explicitly equates living the Christian life with keeping the feast of unleavened bread — the ongoing expulsion of the old leaven of the pre-redemption life.
7: No leavened bread to be seen in all your territory during the seven days — the thorough removal of the old. Theme: the new covenant calls for the complete removal of what corrupts — the old humanity that belongs to pre-redemption existence (Ephesians 4:22-24: 'put off your old self... put on the new self'). As the household is cleansed of every trace of leaven before the feast, so the redeemed life is cleansed of every trace of the old life. The domestic act of removing leaven is the household form of the theological reality of sanctification.
8: 'It is because of what YHWH did for me when I came out of Egypt' — the first-person testimony at the Passover feast. Theme: the personal appropriation of the redemption event. The annual rehearsal of the Exodus is not abstract history but personal testimony: I was enslaved; I was delivered. The NT parallel is the personal testimony of faith: 'I have been crucified with Christ and I no longer live, but Christ lives in me' (Galatians 2:20). The feast structures the worshiper's identity around the redemption event, making it 'mine' in every generation.
9: The feast as a sign on your hand and a memorial between your eyes — a teaching about identity. This metaphor is later literalized in the phylacteries (tefillin) worn by Jewish men at prayer. Theme: the redemption event must shape the hands (action) and the mind (thought) of the covenant people. The NT equivalent is Paul's appeal: 'offer your bodies as a living sacrifice' (Romans 12:1) and 'be transformed by the renewing of your mind' (Romans 12:2). The hand and the forehead — action and thought — are to be marked by the redemption in both covenants.
10: Keep this statute at its appointed time year by year — the annual rhythm of memorial. Theme: the regular, repeated nature of corporate worship as the mechanism by which the community's identity is maintained across generations. The Lord's Supper is similarly recurring — 'as often as you eat this bread and drink this cup' (1 Corinthians 11:26). Redemption is not a one-time cognitive event but a regularly re-inhabited story that forms the community in every generation.
11: When YHWH brings you into the land he swore to give — the connection of the feast to the covenant promise. Theme: the redemption event and the inheritance are a single package. The Lord's Supper is similarly eschatological — 'until he comes' (1 Corinthians 11:26) points to the final inheritance. As the Passover feast looked forward to the promised land, the Lord's Supper looks forward to the marriage supper of the Lamb (Revelation 19:9). Both are meals of already-and-not-yet: the redemption has happened; the full inheritance is still coming.
12: Set apart every firstborn that opens the womb — the practical implementation of Exodus 13:2. A type: the firstborn male of every womb is consecrated to YHWH because YHWH killed Egypt's firstborn and spared Israel's. In the OT the firstborn is the priestly representative of the family — the one who stands before YHWH on behalf of the household. Christ fulfills this as the great High Priest (Hebrews 7:25-26) who stands before the Father as the consecrated firstborn on behalf of all who are in him.
13: The firstborn donkey redeemed with a lamb; if not redeemed, break its neck — no middle path. A type: the principle of substitutionary redemption. The lamb dies so the donkey lives. Every clean animal substitution in the OT sacrificial system points forward to the Lamb of God whose substitution requires no further redemption (Hebrews 10:14: 'by one sacrifice he has made perfect forever those who are being made holy'). The donkey that would otherwise be killed is redeemed not by anything in itself but by the lamb given in its place — precisely the logic of Galatians 3:13 (Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us).
14: 'When your son asks you in times to come, what is this? you shall say to him...' — the intergenerational transmission of the redemption narrative. A theme: the covenant community's responsibility to tell the story to the next generation. Jesus instructs his disciples to 'do this in remembrance of me' — the Lord's Supper is the mechanism by which the story of redemption is told to every subsequent generation through enacted proclamation: 'you proclaim the Lord's death until he comes' (1 Corinthians 11:26). The question-and-answer catechism of the Passover (haggadah) is the model for all Christian teaching of the gospel.
15: YHWH killed every firstborn in Egypt and therefore the firstborn of humans are redeemed — the equivalence establishes the price: life for life. Theme: in Christ, the true Firstborn gives his life so that all who are 'in him' may be counted as redeemed. Romans 8:29: those God foreknew he predestined to be conformed to the likeness of his Son, that he might be the firstborn among many brothers. The firstborn gives life to the many who follow — the resurrection of Christ is the means by which the many firstborn-by-grace receive their redemption.
16: The sign on the hand and the frontlet between the eyes — the bodily markers of the covenant identity. Theme: the new covenant marks its members in parallel ways — the seal of the Holy Spirit (Ephesians 1:13-14: 'you were marked in him with a seal, the promised Holy Spirit') and baptism as the bodily sign of belonging to Christ. The forehead as a place of identity-marking recurs in Revelation: the 144,000 have the Father's name written on their foreheads (Revelation 14:1); the servants of God are sealed on their foreheads (Revelation 7:3). The physical body marks the covenantal identity in both Testaments.
17: God did not lead Israel by the short coastal route to Canaan (the Way of the Philistines) because if they faced war they might turn back. Revelation of God: the divine route is calibrated to the community's spiritual capacity, not merely to geographical efficiency. Jesus leads his followers through the wilderness of suffering and testing rather than bypassing it (Acts 14:22: 'we must go through many hardships to enter the kingdom of God'). The circuitous route is not divine inattention but divine pedagogy — the shorter path leads to retreat; the longer path leads to formation.
18: God led Israel around by the way of the wilderness toward the Red Sea — the wilderness is the curriculum. A shadow: Jesus' forty days in the wilderness (Matthew 4:1-11) recapitulate Israel's forty years. The wilderness is where faith is tested and revealed — where dependence on God is established not as a theological principle but as a lived reality. The route through the wilderness is the only route that produces the covenant fidelity YHWH is seeking. Paul in 1 Corinthians 10:1-13 draws the wilderness experience as a warning and model for the church.
19: Moses took the bones of Joseph — the covenant promise carried forward across death. Genesis 50:25 records Joseph's deathbed request: 'God will surely come to your aid, and then you must carry my bones up from this place.' The carrying of Joseph's bones is the fulfillment of a deathbed faith-confession; it is also a type of resurrection hope — the bones that went down into Egypt go back up. Hebrews 11:22: 'By faith Joseph, when his end was near, spoke about the exodus of the Israelites and gave instructions concerning the burial of his bones.' Joseph's faith looked forward to precisely this moment.
20: They set out from Succoth and camped at Etham on the edge of the wilderness — the first stages of the journey toward the promised land. Theme: the covenant community's life as a pilgrimage through the wilderness of this age toward the inheritance. 1 Peter 2:11 addresses believers as 'foreigners and exiles'; Hebrews 13:14: 'here we do not have an enduring city, but we are looking for the city that is to come.' The journey through the wilderness is not an unfortunate delay but the fundamental shape of the Christian life until the return of Christ.
21: The pillar of cloud by day and pillar of fire by night — a type of the Holy Spirit's guidance. The cloud that guided Israel through the wilderness is connected in the NT to Spirit-language in two ways. First, 1 Corinthians 10:2 says Israel 'was baptized into Moses in the cloud and in the sea' — the cloud is part of the baptism-type. Second, the pillar that stands between Israel and Egypt (Exodus 14:19-20) is the same cloud that leads them — the Spirit guides and protects simultaneously. Jesus promises a Paraclete who will guide the community through its own wilderness (John 16:13: 'when he, the Spirit of truth, comes, he will guide you into all truth').
22: The pillar of cloud by day and pillar of fire by night never departed — the continuous divine presence with the covenant community. Theme: the perpetual presence of the Spirit with the church. Jesus promises: 'I am with you always, to the very end of the age' (Matthew 28:20). The pillar that never departed from Israel in the wilderness is the promise of divine accompaniment that the NT claims is fulfilled permanently in the indwelling Spirit: 'I will ask the Father, and he will give you another Paraclete to be with you forever' (John 14:16). The pillar that was visible in the wilderness becomes the invisible but permanent Spirit in the new covenant age.
